guest: Andrew Wilkinson, founder of MetaLab and Tiny, dubbed 'the Warren Buffett of the internet'

business_ideas.idea: Capital Daily: a well-funded local news operation for Victoria, BC as a repeatable local-media playbook
Mailman: email firewall/scheduler that batches inbox delivery for focus
Buy boring profitable internet businesses cheaply and install great operators (the Tiny model)

predictions.text: Remote work will permanently reprice where people live and what offices are worth
Local news rebuilt as lean digital-first operations can work in city after city

disagreements: Philosophy clash: the hosts romanticize innovation and swinging big, while Andrew explicitly rejects the miserable-genius path in favor of boring, cash-flowing businesses and a nice life.
dynamics: Reverent but probing — the hosts treat Andrew as a mentor figure and mine him for the holding-company playbook. Andrew is calm and deliberate against Sam's ambition-heavy energy; Shaan extracts the mechanics.
frameworks: Buffett-for-internet model: buy wonderful boring businesses at fair prices, hold forever
Operator leverage: the constraint is finding great CEOs, then delegating with clear incentives
Anti-goal lifestyle design: choose businesses by the life they produce, not just the upside
learnings: Risk-adjusted, a portfolio of dull profitable companies can beat chasing one moonshot
Sourcing and retaining operators — not deal flow — is the bottleneck of the holdco model
Deciding what you actually want daily life to feel like should precede company selection

banger_quotes.quote: Rather own 10 car dealerships and sit on them for 50 years, live a nice life

numbers_dropped: Tiny: roughly 30 wholly-owned companies plus dozens of minority investments
Capital Daily: ~40K subscribers in a ~200K-person city (~20% penetration)
notable: The episode crystallizes the Buffett-vs-Andreessen life choice: quality of life compounding versus world-changing innovation.
